topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped brick. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles in my neck strain. I clicked it open, the subject line a mocking mix of my title and someone else’s name. A slow, hot flush crept up my chest. I reread the text three times, my eyes blurring slightly as I tried to reconcile the words—my meticulously researched analysis, my unique perspective—with the author listed as some stranger named 'Alex Vance.' I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet office. I stood up, pacing the small space between my desk and the window, running a hand through my already messy hair. What kind of utter disrespect was this? I started barking into my phone, the words coming out clipped and sharp, a tight, strained sound that barely resembled coherent speech.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat, a sudden, cold wave washing over me. It wasn't a shock; it was a slow, creeping realization that tasted like ash. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight as I processed the audacity of it all. My hands began to tremble, a fine vibration that made the keyboard feel slippery beneath my fingertips. I rubbed my temples vigorously, trying to physically push the confusing facts away. "No," I whispered to the empty room, the sound thin and brittle. I began methodically scrolling through the publication details, my gaze darting from one piece of evidence to the next, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the desk. Every line seemed deliberately placed to wound, and the sheer, baffling incompetence of the situation made my stomach clench with a tight, unpleasant knot.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I physically recoiled. It was like being slapped across the face with a wet towel. I let out a long, slow sigh, the air escaping my lungs in a rush that felt too loud. I didn't move immediately; instead, I just sat there, perfectly still, absorbing the sheer absurdity of the situation. A slow, almost imperceptible shake ran through my shoulders. I picked up the printed draft, the paper feeling suddenly flimsy and meaningless in my hands. My mouth felt dry, and I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes. I picked up my phone, my fingers moving with a jerky, agitated energy as I began dialing, my voice pitched low and dangerously steady as I prepared to confront the editorial team.
